%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 3.9-3 Relationship Between BIBO and Asymptotic Stability

> **Section Objective:** Decide when BIBO stability and asymptotic stability agree, and when they do not.

---

**Concepts In This Section:**

- characteristic root location
- absolute summability of modes
- asymptotic stability implies BIBO stability
- BIBO stability may hide internal instability
- controllable and observable equivalence

## 1. Why the unit circle decides BIBO stability

Every discrete-time characteristic mode takes the form \(\gamma_k^n\), where \(\gamma_k\) is a characteristic root and \(n\) is the time index. The formula above shows that the infinite sum of the mode's absolute values converges to a finite number only when \(|\gamma_k| < 1\) — that is, when the root lies strictly inside the unit circle in the gamma plane. Here \(|\gamma_k|\) is the root's distance from the origin.

**Exam trigger:** When asked about BIBO stability from characteristic roots, check whether every relevant mode is absolutely summable.

**Common misuse:** Treating roots exactly on the unit circle as BIBO-stable. A root with \(|\gamma_k| = 1\) does not decay in magnitude, so the geometric sum diverges — it is not absolutely summable.

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}|\gamma_k|^n=\frac{1}{1-|\gamma_k|},\quad |\gamma_k|<1$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="exam_pattern_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Root location in the gamma plane determines absolute summability. Only roots strictly inside the unit circle produce BIBO-stable modes.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1781408597205-1932.png)

## 2. The one-way implication

### ROOT LOCATION CLASSIFICATION

Use root magnitude — not sign — to classify each mode:

- **\(|\gamma_k| < 1\) (inside the unit circle):** Mode decays to zero. Absolutely summable. Contributes to BIBO stability.
- **\(|\gamma_k| > 1\) (outside the unit circle):** Mode grows without bound. Not absolutely summable. System is BIBO-unstable.
- **\(|\gamma_k| = 1\) (on the unit circle):** Mode neither grows nor decays. Not absolutely summable. Fails BIBO stability.

#### Minimal Example

Roots \(-0.5\) and \(-2\): since \(|-2| = 2 > 1\), the system is **BIBO-unstable and asymptotically unstable**.

Roots \(0.3\) and \(0.7\): since \(|0.3| < 1\) and \(|0.7| < 1\), both modes decay — the system is **BIBO-stable and asymptotically stable**.

#### Note

In discrete time, the unit circle is the stability boundary. This replaces the left-half-plane boundary used in continuous-time analysis.

## 3. When the two stability tests become equivalent

If every internal characteristic mode decays — meaning all roots satisfy \(|\gamma_k| < 1\) — then the impulse response is absolutely summable, and every bounded input produces a bounded output. This is the safe one-way arrow.

- **Asymptotically stable** means the internal natural response dies out as \(n \to \infty\).
- **BIBO stable** means every bounded input \(x[n]\) produces a bounded output \(y[n]\).

**When to use this:** If a problem gives internal characteristic roots and asks about external bounded-input behavior, internal decay guarantees BIBO stability.

**Common misuse:** Do not reverse the arrow automatically. BIBO stability alone may miss hidden internal modes that are not visible from the external input-output description.

$$\text{asymptotically stable}\Rightarrow\text{BIBO stable}$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="trap_exposure" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 The measured impulse response looks stable, but S2 contains a hidden root at \(\gamma = 2\) that grows internally. External BIBO stability does not reveal this.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1781408754917-8626.png)

$$h[n]=h_1[n]*h_2[n]=(0.5)^n u[n]$$
### EXAMPLE 3.26 — The Hidden-Mode Trap

The cascade of \(S_1\) and \(S_2\) produces an external composite impulse response \(h[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]\). Because \(|0.5| < 1\), this sequence is absolutely summable, so the black-box input-output behavior is **BIBO-stable**.

However, subsystem \(S_2\) internally contains the mode \(2^n u[n]\), whose characteristic root is \(\gamma = 2\). Since \(|2| > 1\), this internal mode grows without bound — the system is **asymptotically unstable** internally.

The cancellation between \(S_1\) and \(S_2\) hides the unstable root from the external measurement.

**Exam trigger:** If a problem asks about internal stability, inspect hidden characteristic roots or state variables — not only the external impulse response.

**Common misuse:** Concluding asymptotic stability solely from a stable-looking transfer function or impulse response.

## 3. When the two stability tests become equivalent

The internal and external descriptions of an LTID system agree only when the system is **controllable and observable**. At the level needed here: a system is controllable and observable when no internal mode can be isolated from the input-output description — there are no hidden modes.

When this condition holds, every internal characteristic root is visible in the external impulse response, so BIBO stability and asymptotic stability carry exactly the same information.

**When to use this:** Unless a problem explicitly warns about uncontrollable or unobservable dynamics, the textbook assumes the two descriptions are equivalent, and you may treat the double arrow as valid.

**Common misuse:** Applying this equivalence to a system like Example 3.26, where an unstable internal mode is hidden by cancellation and the system is not fully observable or controllable.

$$\text{BIBO stable}\Longleftrightarrow\text{asymptotically stable}\quad\text{when the LTID system is controllable and observable}$$

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Mode \(\gamma_k^n\) is absolutely summable only when \(|\gamma_k|<1\): \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}|\gamma_k|^n=\frac{1}{1-|\gamma_k|}\). Roots on or outside the unit circle fail BIBO stability.
- The safe one-way arrow: \(\text{asymptotically stable}\Rightarrow\text{BIBO stable}\). Do not reverse it without checking for hidden internal modes.
- Example 3.26 trap: \(h[n]=h_1[n]*h_2[n]=(0.5)^n u[n]\) is BIBO-stable externally, but \(S_2\) hides root \(\gamma=2\) — internally unstable.
- Full equivalence \(\text{BIBO stable}\Longleftrightarrow\text{asymptotically stable}\) holds only when the LTID system is controllable and observable.

*Next, we use characteristic roots to build more intuition about discrete-time system behavior.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
